838	Allowed `external_default` values:
839	
840	- `disabled`
841	- `ask`
842	- `enabled`

They require explicit policy grants by `ToolID`, toolset, source, or agent plus the effective ACP/session ceiling, approval bridge when required, session lineage, and hook revalidation.

848	### Existing MCP Config And Auth Lifecycle
849	
850	No new `config.toml` keys are added for remote MCP OAuth tokens. Existing MCP lifecycle remains authoritative:
851	
852	- MCP server definitions continue to come from top-level `[mcp_servers]`, provider `[providers.<name>.mcp_servers]`, agent-local `mcp_servers`, global/workspace `mcp.json`, skill sidecars, and extension `resources.mcp_servers`.
853	- Remote MCP auth configuration continues to use `MCPAuthConfig` fields (`type`, metadata/issuer/authorization/token/revocation URLs, `client_id`, `client_secret_env`, `scopes`) on remote MCP servers.
854	- Access tokens and refresh tokens continue to live only in the `internal/mcp/auth` token store backed by `internal/store/globaldb`; they are not copied into registry config, session lineage, tool descriptors, events, or extension manifests.
855	- The registry may read redacted MCP auth status to produce operator diagnostics and availability reason codes, but cannot mutate auth state. Login, refresh, and logout remain `agh mcp auth ...` operations in MVP.
856	- The hosted MCP session bind token is ephemeral process/session state, not `config.toml` state and not part of the MCP OAuth token store.

858	### Agent Definitions
859	
860	Keep the existing `tools` field but harden its meaning:
861	
862	- `tools`: exact canonical `ToolID`s or approved wildcard patterns.
863	- `toolsets`: named toolset IDs.
864	- `deny_tools`: exact IDs or patterns that always narrow permissions.
865	
866	Session lineage should persist concrete resolved `ToolID` atoms, not broad unresolved wildcard patterns. Child session permissions must remain subsets of parent session permissions.
867	
868	Invalid existing lineage atoms reject session spawn/load with a typed validation error. Greenfield posture applies: AGH does not silently normalize old atoms, and local databases that predate this TechSpec require a fresh `AGH_HOME` rather than compatibility migration shims.
869	
870	### Tool Pattern Grammar
871	
872	Allowed policy pattern forms:
873	
874	- exact canonical IDs, for example `agh__skill_view`;
875	- namespace-prefix wildcards ending in `*`, for example `agh__skill_*` or `mcp__github__*`;
876	- toolset IDs in `toolsets`, never in `tools`.
877	
878	Disallowed forms:
879	
880	- regular expressions;
881	- suffix wildcards such as `*__search`;
882	- mid-segment wildcards such as `agh__*__view`;
883	- uppercase, dots, hyphens, or empty segments;
884	- wildcard forms that would match across a reserved `__` boundary ambiguously.
885	
886	Pattern matching runs against canonical `ToolID` only. Display titles, raw MCP tool names, and extension manifest names do not participate in policy matching.
887

889	
890	Config validation must reject:
891	
892	- invalid `ToolID` patterns;
893	- unknown toolset IDs when a config is resolved in a concrete workspace;
894	- `__` misuse;
895	- extension attempts to publish under reserved `agh__*`;
896	- global defaults that would expose external tools without source policy support;
897	- result byte limits below zero or above a daemon maximum.

1027	9. Add TypeScript SDK `extension.tool(...)`, schema digesting, and handler registration - depends on step 8.
1028	10. Add public Go extension SDK with function-based tool helpers equivalent to TypeScript SDK - depends on step 8.

1030	12. Add hosted MCP stdio proxy command `agh tool mcp --session`, session-bound token bind, approval bridge, and existing MCP resource/auth preservation - depends on steps 3-6 and 11.

1036	### Technical Dependencies
1037	
1038	Blocking dependencies that must be resolved before implementation:
1039	
1040	- Existing ACP `permissions.mode` behavior in `internal/acp/permission.go`.
1041	- Existing session lineage permission atoms in `internal/store/session_lineage.go`.
1042	- Existing extension resource publication and `resources.publish.families = ["tools"]`.
1043	- Existing extension subprocess lifecycle, JSON-RPC `process.Call`, Host API capability checks, and TypeScript `Extension.handle(...)` handler pattern.
1044	- Existing hooks payload system.
1045	- Existing MCP server configuration/resource lifecycle in `internal/config/provider.go`, `internal/config/mcpjson.go`, `internal/config/mcp_resource.go`, `internal/skills/mcp.go`, and `internal/extension/resource_publication.go`.
1046	- Existing MCP auth lifecycle in `internal/mcp/auth`, `internal/store/globaldb/global_db_mcp_auth.go`, `internal/cli/mcp_auth.go`, `internal/settings`, and `internal/daemon/settings.go`.
1047	- Existing skills registry APIs.
1048	- Existing task/network services.
1049	- OpenAPI/codegen path for web contract updates.

1051	### Safety Invariants
1052	
1053	1. Every AGH-owned tool call enters `internal/tools.Registry.Call`; CLI, HTTP, UDS, hosted MCP, extension, and session paths cannot bypass the registry dispatch pipeline.
1054	2. Dispatch recomputes availability and `EffectiveToolDecision` at call time, even when discovery already filtered the tool.
1055	3. ACP `permissions.mode` is always a ceiling. Registry, source, agent, session, and hook policy can narrow authority but cannot raise it above the effective ACP/session mode.
1056	4. `approve-all` skips approval prompts only for otherwise allowed tools; it does not bypass explicit denies, source grants, session lineage, conflicts, unavailable backends, or hooks.
1057	5. `approve-reads` applies only to registry-classified read-only tools. Mutating, destructive, open-world, network-send, and task-write tools cannot inherit read approval by display title or ACP kind.
1058	6. Session lineage stores concrete canonical `ToolID` atoms after toolset expansion; child sessions can only receive a subset of parent concrete atoms.
1059	7. Tool ID collisions fail closed. A conflicted tool is operator-visible with reason codes and absent from session/model-visible projections.
1060	8. Extension-installed tools can become executable in MVP only when the manifest-authoritative descriptor, source policy, extension health, runtime `provide_tools` descriptor, and `tools/call` handler all agree.
1061	9. Third-party extension tool handlers never run in-process in the daemon during MVP; TypeScript and Go function handlers run inside supervised extension subprocesses.
1062	10. Hooks dispatch at the call site and cannot tail event tables, spawn parallel queues, or mutate durable ownership state outside typed hook contracts.
1063	11. Tool result limiting and redaction run before results cross CLI, HTTP, UDS, MCP, SSE, logs, memory, or event payloads.
1064	12. Raw `claim_token`, MCP auth tokens, OAuth codes, PKCE verifiers, and secret bindings never appear in tool inputs/outputs persisted or emitted by AGH-owned surfaces.
1065	13. Hosted MCP `tools/list` is a projection of `GET /api/sessions/{id}/tools`; divergence is a test failure.
1066	14. Operator-visible diagnostics are not security boundaries. Hidden session projection plus dispatch-time revalidation is the security boundary.
1067	15. Toolsets are expanded deterministically and cycle-checked before session projection; recursive expansion cannot happen lazily during dispatch.
1068	16. Hosted MCP cannot bind to a session without a daemon-minted, single-use, session-bound proxy token. The raw token is redacted like `claim_token`, never logged, never accepted from client tool input, and invalidated on bind/session end/disconnect/TTL.
1069	17. Hosted MCP `tools/call` cannot pass an `approval_required` decision unless the daemon completes an ACP/session-mediated approval equivalent to CLI/HTTP/UDS approval semantics.
1070	18. No `agh__task_*` tool may bypass `task.Service.ClaimNextRun`, `Spawn`, session manager terminal-state authority, or task lifecycle authority. Claim/release/complete/fail/run-start operations are excluded from MVP tools.
1071	19. External `extension_host` and `mcp` backend tools are executable only through their registered runtime handles; missing handlers, missing MCP clients, missing capabilities, source denies, auth failures, or runtime mismatches fail closed before user code or remote tools run.
1072	20. Remote MCP OAuth/PKCE credentials are owned only by `internal/mcp/auth` and its `TokenStore`; the registry may consume redacted status and call through a narrow `internal/mcp/auth`-owned interface, but cannot persist, log, refresh, revoke, or copy access/refresh tokens. Raw tokens stay out of descriptors, resources, events, API responses, CLI output, MCP responses, and tool results.
1073	21. Hosted MCP bind tokens and remote MCP OAuth tokens have separate issuers, storage, lifetimes, redaction labels, and failure codes. A `hosted_mcp_bind_token` must never satisfy a remote MCP server auth check, and a remote MCP OAuth token must never bind an AGH hosted MCP proxy.

1134	## Technical Considerations
1135	
1136	### Key Decisions
1137	
1138	Decision: Tool Registry is an AGH daemon runtime service, not an ACP registry.
1139	
1140	Rationale: ACP has no callable tool registry and relies on MCP for tool discovery.
1141	
1142	Trade-off: AGH must maintain its own registry semantics and expose them through MCP/session surfaces.
1143	
1144	Decision: Use one canonical `ToolID` with `__` namespace separators.
1145	
1146	Rationale: Avoid dotted/internal plus wire alias ambiguity and stay compatible with stricter provider naming limits.
1147	
1148	Trade-off: `agh__skill_view` is less visually elegant than `agh.skill.view`, but it prevents dual identity bugs.
1149	
1150	Decision: Built-ins execute in-process; TypeScript and Go extension tools execute out-of-process through `extension_host`.
1151	
1152	Rationale: Daemon safety and extension isolation matter more than plugin convenience.
1153	
1154	Trade-off: Extension tool latency and setup are higher, but failure containment is better and extension authors still get function-based APIs.
1155	
1156	Decision: Remote MCP-backed tools execute in MVP through daemon-owned MCP clients.
1157	
1158	Rationale: MCP config/auth already exists and the registry must not become useful only for descriptors.
1159	
1160	Trade-off: The MVP must implement stricter auth redaction and adapter tests, but operators get one coherent tool model.
1161	
1162	Decision: ACP `permissions.mode` is the approval ceiling.
1163	
1164	Rationale: Avoid contradictory policy layers and keep existing settings truthful.
1165	
1166	Trade-off: Registry policy must compute effective decisions rather than simple allow/deny flags.
1167	
1168	Decision: Operator and session projections differ.
1169	
1170	Rationale: Operators need diagnostics; models should see only callable tools.
1171	
1172	Trade-off: More projection logic, but less model confusion and fewer unsafe calls.
1173	
1174	Decision: Keep cold resource records separate from runtime handles.
1175	
1176	Rationale: Resource/projector system is good for installed metadata; executable dispatch needs function/protocol handles and live health.
1177	
1178	Trade-off: More types, but cleaner lifecycle and safer extension support.
1179	
1180	### Known Risks
1181	
1182	Risk: Mutating tools mislabeled as read-only.
1183	
1184	Mitigation: Descriptor validation, review tests, policy matrix tests, and hook/audit visibility.
1185	
1186	Risk: External tool name collisions force late breaking changes.
1187	
1188	Mitigation: Enforce canonical `ToolID` grammar and fail-closed collision handling in MVP.
1189	
1190	Risk: Hosted MCP path diverges from CLI/HTTP/UDS behavior.
1191	
1192	Mitigation: Hosted MCP proxies through UDS into the same registry dispatch path.
1193	
1194	Risk: Extension tools become visible before safe runtime execution is confirmed.
1195	
1196	Mitigation: Operator-visible only until manifest/runtime reconciliation, availability, source policy, and backend handle all pass.
1197	
1198	Risk: `approve-all` is misunderstood as "execute everything."
1199	
1200	Mitigation: Docs and UI copy must clarify it auto-approves otherwise allowed calls; it does not bypass explicit denies, source grants, availability, lineage, or hooks.
1201	
1202	Risk: Toolsets create ambiguous policy language.
1203	
1204	Mitigation: Keep `tools` and `toolsets` as separate typed fields; expand toolsets to concrete `ToolID`s for session lineage.
1205	
1206	Risk: Result payloads leak secrets or overwhelm context.
1207	
1208	Mitigation: Central result limiter, redaction metadata, digesting, and output budgets.

1	# Tool Registry Synthesis and Proposed Direction
2	
3	## Executive Summary
4	
5	AGH should build a Tool Registry as a daemon-owned runtime service that composes tool metadata, availability, policy, execution, hooks, telemetry, and extension adapters.
6	
7	The foundation should not be "add many built-in tools." The foundation should be:
8	
9	1. a first-class runtime tool contract,
10	2. a registry that aggregates built-ins, MCP, extensions, and dynamic providers,
11	3. a policy/availability projection for each agent/session,
12	4. a single dispatch pipeline,
13	5. an extension-safe execution boundary,
14	6. agent-manageable CLI/HTTP/UDS/session surfaces,
15	7. toolsets/bundles comparable to skills.
16	
17	The ACP inventory found `.resources/openfang` present, but with no meaningful ACP evidence.
18	
19	## Recommended Architecture
20	
21	### Accepted decisions so far
22	
23	- Extension tool execution boundary: manifest-first descriptors with out-of-process execution only for extension tools in the MVP.
24	- Session exposure path: AGH-hosted local MCP server plus shared CLI/HTTP/UDS contracts.
25	- Package boundary: `internal/tools` owns runtime registry contracts and dispatch; a thin `internal/catalog` facade composes tools and skills for cross-domain discovery.
26	- MVP native tool scope: bootstrap catalog/skill tools plus network and bounded task tools (`agh__tool_*`, `agh__skill_*`, `agh__network_peers`, `agh__network_send`, `agh__task_*`).
27	- Policy integration: existing ACP `permissions.mode` is the system/session approval ceiling; registry policy is a granular layer below it and cannot silently grant more authority than ACP policy allows.
28	- Visibility by surface: operator surfaces show unavailable/unauthorized/conflicted tools with reason codes; session/model-visible surfaces expose only tools callable in that effective session context.
29	- Naming/collision policy: one canonical public `ToolID` uses provider-safe lower snake segments separated by reserved `__`, for example `agh__skill_view` and `mcp__github__create_issue`; this is captured in ADR-007.
30	
31	### ACP compatibility finding
32	
33	ACP does not define a durable callable-tool registry. It defines session lifecycle, `mcpServers` bootstrap fields, client authority callbacks, permission requests, and observable tool-call events. ACP `ToolCall` records have `toolCallId`, human-readable `title`, coarse `kind`, status, locations, raw input/output, and content, but no programmatic tool `name` equivalent to MCP `Tool.name`.
34	
35	This means the Tool Registry should remain an AGH daemon/runtime service. Session exposure should use the accepted AGH-hosted MCP path, where AGH exposes the canonical `ToolID` directly as the hosted MCP `Tool.name`.
36	
37	Accepted identity format:
38	
39	- `ToolID`: stable provider-safe id with reserved `__` namespace separators, such as `agh__skill_view`.
40	- `DisplayTitle`: human-readable and non-unique.
41	- `SourceRef`: structured provenance, not inferred only from prefixes.
42	
43	Collision handling must be fail-closed. Canonical ID collisions are provider registration errors or operator diagnostics. Sanitized external-name collisions make the affected tools unavailable to that session until resolved. Display title collisions are allowed because titles are not policy identities.
44	
45	### 1. Split descriptor, runtime handle, and resource record
46	
47	Keep the existing `internal/tools.Tool` resource shape as the cold catalog/desired-state record, but introduce a runtime contract with separate types:
48	
49	- `ToolID`: stable provider-safe id such as `agh__skill_view`, `mcp__github__create_issue`, `ext__linear__search`.
50	- `Descriptor`: identity, description, input schema, optional output schema, read-only/destructive/open-world/concurrency metadata, source/provenance, visibility, tags, owner, result budget.
51	- `Handle`: descriptor plus `Availability(ctx, ToolContext)` and `Call(ctx, ToolCall)` for executable tools.
52	- `Provider`: contributes descriptors/handles and can refresh.
53	- `Registry`: owns provider registration, indexing, listing, search, policy projection, and dispatch.
54	- `ToolResult`: structured output, preview, artifacts, redactions, bytes, display title, metadata.
55	
56	This avoids overloading the desired-state resource with function pointers while still allowing resource records to feed the runtime registry.
57	
58	### 2. Use manifest-first extension tools
59	
60	Extension manifests should continue to declare tool metadata statically. Add enough metadata to connect the declaration to a backend:
61	
62	- backend kind: `mcp`, `extension_host`, `subprocess`, or `builtin` where appropriate;
63	- namespace/owner;
64	- visibility;
65	- risk class;
66	- required config/env/capabilities;
67	- optional toolset memberships.
68	
69	The registry can list these tools without executing extension code. A tool becomes executable only when its backend adapter is healthy, authorized, and has a callable handle.
70	
71	Recommendation for MVP: no in-process third-party handlers. Built-in Go tools can register in-process. Extension tools should execute through MCP or an extension sidecar/Host API adapter.
72	
73	### 3. Make availability a state machine, not a boolean
74	
75	Use explicit status:
76	
77	- `registered`: descriptor exists.
78	- `enabled`: operator/session policy has not disabled it.
79	- `available`: dependencies are present and backend is healthy.
80	- `authorized`: caller policy permits visibility/use.
81	- `executable`: there is a live handle for dispatch.
82	- `conflicted`: id/name collision requires resolution.
83	
84	Discovery can hide unavailable/unauthorized tools from agents while operator surfaces show reasons. Dispatch must recheck availability and authorization.
85	
86	The registry should expose separate operator and session projections. The operator projection includes diagnostics, source/provenance, policy reasons, availability reasons, and conflicts. The session projection powers hosted MCP and future driver injection and includes only tools that pass effective visibility/execution gates for that session.
87	
88	### 4. Centralize dispatch
89	
90	Every AGH-owned tool call should pass through:
91	
92	1. resolve tool id/alias in context,
93	2. validate input against schema,
94	3. compute availability,
95	4. evaluate policy and session permission atoms,
96	5. run `tool.pre_call` hooks,
97	6. enforce concurrency/rate/result budgets,
98	7. call provider adapter,
99	8. normalize result,
100	9. redact/truncate/persist output,
101	10. run `tool.post_call` or `tool.post_error` hooks,
102	11. emit telemetry.
103	
104	No CLI, HTTP, UDS, MCP, extension, or session path should bypass this pipeline.
105	
106	### 5. Model policy as overlays
107	
108	Use one policy engine that combines:
109	
110	- system/session ACP `permissions.mode`,
111	- daemon defaults,
112	- workspace config,
113	- extension grants,
114	- agent definition,
115	- session lineage `SessionPermissionPolicy.Tools`,
116	- skill/command scoped grants where relevant,
117	- explicit allow/deny patterns,
118	- named toolsets,
119	- risk defaults.
120	
121	Toolsets should be recursive resources/config entries. This copies Hermes' strongest idea while fitting AGH's resource model.
122	
123	The registry must not create a second approval system that contradicts ACP. `approve-all` removes automatic approval prompts for otherwise allowed tools, but explicit registry denies, source grants, session lineage restrictions, availability failures, and hooks still apply. `approve-reads` auto-approves only registry-classified read-only tools. `deny-all` denies execution by default and requires an explicit approval path.
124	
125	### 6. Provide a small bootstrap native toolset
126	
127	The TechSpec should not enumerate every future AGH tool. It should require a small proving set:
128	
129	- `agh__tool_list`
130	- `agh__tool_search`
131	- `agh__tool_info`
132	- `agh__skill_list`
133	- `agh__skill_view`
134	
135	Optional later groups:
136	
137	- `agh__skill_install`
138	- `agh__network_peers`
139	- `agh__network_send`
140	- `agh__task_*`
141	- `agh__extension_*`
142	
143	The bootstrap set proves discovery, schema loading, skill body loading, policy, result budget, and telemetry without overcommitting the whole daemon.
144	
145	### 7. Expose agent-manageable surfaces
146	
147	The registry should have shared contract types used by:
148	
149	- CLI: `agh tool list/search/info/invoke`.
150	- HTTP: `/api/tools`, `/api/tools/{id}`, `/api/tools/{id}/invoke`.
151	- UDS: same operations for local agents and internal tools.
152	- Session-visible tool surface: either an AGH-hosted MCP server, driver-specific ACP tool injection where possible, or a fallback where agents can use `agh` CLI/UDS through their runtime.
153	
154	The TechSpec should pick one MVP path and keep the others as contract-compatible surfaces.
155	
156	### 8. Treat Tool Search as provider-neutral
157	
158	Claude Code's `tool_reference` mechanism is useful but not portable. AGH should implement registry search as a normal catalog operation first:
159	
160	- search over name, namespace, description, tags, source, toolset, and search hints;
161	- return metadata first;
162	- load schema/details on demand;
163	- optionally persist discovered state per session later.
164	
165	Driver-specific schema-on-demand integration can be a future enhancement.
166	
167	### 9. Reuse existing AGH infrastructure
168	
169	Build on:
170	
171	- `internal/tools` for contracts and registry,
172	- `internal/resources` for desired-state records,
173	- `internal/extension` manifest publication,
174	- `internal/hooks` for pre/post/error dispatch gates,
175	- `internal/store.SessionPermissionPolicy` for lineage constraints,
176	- `internal/skills.Registry` for skill listing/content,
177	- `internal/toolruntime` for subprocess ownership if extension tools need process handles,
178	- `internal/api/contract` for shared HTTP/UDS payloads.
179	
180	Avoid a large generic `internal/catalog` at first unless it only coordinates cross-domain search. The runtime tool registry belongs in or near `internal/tools`; a catalog facade can compose tools and skills for `agh__tool_*` / `agh__skill_*`.
181	
182	## Proposed MVP Scope
183	
184	### In scope
185	
186	- Runtime tool registry contract and central dispatch pipeline.
187	- Built-in provider for `agh__tool_list`, `agh__tool_search`, `agh__tool_info`, `agh__skill_list`, `agh__skill_search`, `agh__skill_view`, `agh__network_peers`, `agh__network_send`, and a bounded `agh__task_*` set.
188	- Resource-backed descriptors from existing `tool` records.
189	- Extension manifest backend metadata for future executable extension tools.
190	- MCP adapter design, even if full MCP call-through is deferred.
191	- Context-specific list/search/info APIs.
192	- Tool policy with allow/deny patterns and named toolsets.
193	- Availability model and reason codes.
194	- Hook integration for pre/post/error.
195	- Telemetry events for list/search/info/call and failures.
196	- CLI/HTTP/UDS contract surfaces.
197	
198	### Out of scope for MVP
199	
200	- Full shell/browser/file tool replacement for ACP runtimes.
201	- Provider-specific Anthropic `tool_reference` integration.
202	- In-process third-party extension handlers.
203	- Large catalog of AGH-native tools beyond the selected catalog/skill/network/task set.
204	- Skill install/remove/update tools unless explicitly paired with supply-chain policy/scanning work.
205	- Network peer remote tool execution.
206	- Marketplace signing/trust overhaul, except for explicit risk hooks needed by extension tools.
207	
208	## Critical Decisions Before TechSpec
209	
210	1. Extension execution boundary: out-of-process only, metadata-only first, or trusted in-process handlers.
211	2. Session exposure path: hosted MCP, direct ACP extension, CLI/UDS fallback, or all in phases.
212	3. Package boundary: runtime registry in `internal/tools` with a catalog facade, or a new `internal/catalog` owning tools and skills together.
213	4. MVP tool set: only list/search/info/view, or include mutating install/network/task tools.
214	5. Policy defaults: external tools disabled, ask, or visible-but-not-callable until granted.
215	6. Availability visibility: hide unavailable tools from agents, show unavailable tools with reasons, or configurable by surface.
216	7. Naming/collision policy: accepted in ADR-007. Use one canonical provider-safe `ToolID` with reserved `__` namespace separators, display-only title, structured provenance, and no shadowing or silent sanitized-name collisions.
217	
218	## Competitor Pattern Matrix
219	
220	| Pattern | Hermes | Claude Code | GoClaw | OpenClaw | AGH Recommendation |
221	|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
222	| Single normalized tool contract | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Required |
223	| Runtime executable registry | Yes | Distributed | Yes | Yes | Required |
224	| Manifest-first extension discovery | Partial | Plugin metadata | Partial | Strong | Required |
225	| MCP as adapter | Yes | Strong | Yes | Strong | Required |
226	| Availability gating | Strong discovery | `isEnabled` + MCP state | Policy/lazy checks | Lifecycle state | Required at discovery and dispatch |
227	| Central dispatch | Mostly | Strong | Mostly | Gateway + adapters | Required with no bypass |
228	| Toolsets/bundles | Strong | Policy lists | Groups | Policy groups | Required |
229	| Deferred search | Partial | Strong | Search helpers | Partial | Provider-neutral MVP |
230	| Concurrency metadata | Partial | Strong | Partial | Partial | Required metadata, scheduling can evolve |
231	| Extension in-process handlers | Yes | No native direct tools | Some | Plugin API | Avoid for MVP |
232	
233	## Implementation Shape to Explore in TechSpec
234	
235	```go
236	type Descriptor struct {
237	    ID          ToolID
238	    DisplayName string
239	    Description string
240	    InputSchema  json.RawMessage
241	    OutputSchema json.RawMessage
242	    Source      SourceRef
243	    Visibility  Visibility
244	    Risk        RiskClass
245	    ReadOnly    bool
246	    Destructive bool
247	    OpenWorld   bool
248	    ConcurrencySafe bool
249	    MaxResultBytes int64
250	    Toolsets []string
251	    Tags []string
252	}
253	
254	type Handle interface {
255	    Descriptor() Descriptor
256	    Availability(ctx context.Context, call ToolContext) Availability
257	    Call(ctx context.Context, call ToolCall) (ToolResult, error)
258	}
259	
260	type Provider interface {
261	    ID() string
262	    ListTools(ctx context.Context) ([]Descriptor, error)
263	    Resolve(ctx context.Context, id ToolID) (Handle, bool, error)
264	}
265	
266	type Registry interface {
267	    List(ctx context.Context, scope Scope) ([]ToolView, error)
268	    Search(ctx context.Context, scope Scope, query SearchQuery) ([]ToolView, error)
269	    Get(ctx context.Context, scope Scope, id ToolID) (ToolView, error)
270	    Call(ctx context.Context, scope Scope, req CallRequest) (ToolResult, error)
271	}
272	```
273	
274	The exact Go shape can change, but the separation should hold.
275	
276	## Risks
277	
278	If AGH exposes tool invocation before policy and availability are in place, it will create a broader attack surface than today's ACP-delegated tools.
279	
280	If extension tools can run in-process, one bad extension can compromise the daemon.
281	
282	If the registry only lists resources but does not dispatch, AGH will still lack the "last mile" that motivated the feature.
283	
284	If the registry only works through one surface, agents will not be able to manage it consistently.
285	
286	If name collisions are postponed, extension/MCP adoption will force a breaking change later.
287

1	# AGH Current State: Tool Registry Foundation
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	AGH already has the cold side of a tool catalog, but not the runtime side.
6	`internal/tools` defines a canonical `tool` resource shape and the daemon projects tool records through the generic resources system. Extensions can publish static tool metadata from `extension.toml`. Sessions can also carry concrete permission atoms for tools in lineage metadata.
7	
8	What is missing is the executable registry: a central service that can answer which tools exist for a specific agent/session, whether each tool is available now, whether the caller is allowed to use it, and how to dispatch the call through one uniform pipeline.
9	
10	This matters because AGH's product premise is agent-first manageability. A tool registry is incomplete if it only helps internal Go code list metadata or if each ACP runtime owns a private tool universe that AGH cannot inspect, govern, or extend.
11	
12	## Existing Mechanisms
13	
14	### Tool resource metadata
15	
16	`internal/tools/tool.go` defines `ToolSource` values for `builtin`, `mcp`, `extension`, and `dynamic`, plus a small `Tool` record:
17	
18	- `Name`
19	- `Description`
20	- `InputSchema`
21	- `ReadOnly`
22	- `Source`
23	
24	The only provider interface is:
25	
26	```go
27	type ToolProvider interface {
28	    Tools(ctx context.Context) ([]Tool, error)
29	}
30	```
31	
32	There is no `Call`, `Availability`, `CheckPermission`, `Aliases`, `Namespace`, `IsConcurrencySafe`, `IsDestructive`, `MaxResultBytes`, `Owner`, `Visibility`, or provenance-rich source metadata.
33	
34	### Desired-state resources
35	
36	`internal/tools/resource.go` defines `ToolResourceKind = "tool"` and validates tool records as JSON-object specs with a maximum size of 256 KiB. This is a good base for persisted inventory, desired-state reconciliation, and extension-published tool metadata.
37	
38	The daemon already has a generic `resourceCatalog[T]` in `internal/daemon/tool_mcp_resources.go` and a `newToolProjector` that projects reconciled `tool` records into a daemon-local snapshot. This catalog is descriptive and revisioned, but it is not executable.
39	
40	### Extension-published tools
41	
42	`internal/extension/manifest.go` already lets extensions declare:
43	
44	```go
45	type ResourcesConfig struct {
46	    Tools map[string]ToolConfig `toml:"tools,omitempty" json:"tools,omitempty"`
47	    MCPServers map[string]MCPServerConfig `toml:"mcp_servers,omitempty" json:"mcp_servers,omitempty"`
48	}
49	```
50	
51	`ToolConfig` carries description, input schema, and read-only status. `ResolveManifestToolResources` converts these manifest entries into `toolspkg.Tool` records with `Source = ToolSourceExtension`. The daemon syncer publishes them into the resource graph with source keys like `extension/<name>/tool/<tool>`.
52	
53	This is close to OpenClaw's manifest-first model, but AGH currently stops at metadata. A manifest-declared extension tool is not callable unless some separate ACP/MCP/runtime surface happens to expose it.
54	
55	### MCP resources
56	
57	The same extension manifest can declare MCP servers, and the daemon sync path resolves them into desired-state MCP server resources. Skills can also declare MCP sidecars. This gives AGH a strong candidate adapter for extension tools: manifest tools may be backed by an MCP server, an extension sidecar Host API endpoint, or native AGH built-ins, but all should normalize into one registry contract.
58	
59	### Session permission atoms
60	
61	`internal/store/session_lineage.go` defines `SessionPermissionPolicy` with concrete atoms:
62	
63	- `Tools`
64	- `Skills`
65	- `MCPServers`
66	- `WorkspacePaths`
67	- `NetworkChannels`
68	- `SandboxProfiles`
69	
70	`internal/session/spawn.go` validates child permissions as a subset of parent permissions. This is an important base for runtime tool policy because it is already persisted with session lineage and already participates in spawn delegation.
71	
72	The current agent definition has a flat `Tools []string` field in `internal/config/agent.go`. It lacks allow/deny overlays, named toolsets, namespace patterns, visibility tiers, and risk classes.
73	
74	### Hooks around tool calls
75	
76	`internal/hooks/payloads.go` already defines `ToolPreCallPayload`, `ToolPostCallPayload`, `ToolPostErrorPayload`, `ToolCallPatch`, and `ToolResultPatch`. This is the right policy extension point for a centralized dispatch pipeline:
77	
78	- pre-call hooks can deny or mutate input
79	- post-call hooks can redact or mutate output
80	- post-error hooks can classify or recover failures
81	
82	The current gap is that AGH does not have a single dispatch pipeline that all AGH-owned tools must pass through.
83	
84	### Skills registry contrast
85	
86	`internal/skills.Registry` is much more mature than tools. It has global snapshots, workspace overlays, content loading, verification, install provenance, and `GlobalVersion()` for invalidation. Skills are injected as a static prompt catalog at session start, while tool resources are not exposed as a session-callable registry.
87	
88	The Tool Registry should copy the skills registry's useful properties where they fit: global/workspace overlays, versioned snapshots, resource projection, progressive disclosure, and explicit content/schema loading.
89	
90	## Gaps
91	
92	1. No central runtime registry that owns executable tool handles.
93	2. No agent-facing discovery API for tools.
94	3. No native AGH tools such as `agh__tool_search`, `agh__skill_view`, or `agh__network_send`.
95	4. No availability model for env vars, binaries, MCP health, extension health, workspace scope, or policy state.
96	5. No central permission pipeline for AGH-owned tools.
97	6. No namespace or structured provenance model, so duplicate names would be ambiguous.
98	7. No toolsets or bundles comparable to skills/capabilities.
99	8. No direct extension execution boundary for manifest-declared tools.
100	9. No consistent way to expose tools over CLI, HTTP, UDS, and session-visible agent surfaces.
101	10. No usage telemetry by tool or skill.
102	11. No result-size budget, persistence policy, redaction, or output mapping at registry level.
103	12. The `dynamic` source enum exists but has no producer.
104	
105	## Relevant Code Paths
106	
107	- `internal/tools/tool.go:14-136`: tool source enum, metadata-only `Tool`, and list-only `ToolProvider`.
108	- `internal/tools/resource.go:13-61`: `ToolResourceKind` codec and JSON schema validation.
109	- `internal/daemon/tool_mcp_resources.go:20-122`: generic daemon `resourceCatalog` and `newToolProjector`.
110	- `internal/daemon/tool_mcp_resources.go:620-640`: extension manifest tools are projected into desired-state resources.
111	- `internal/extension/manifest.go:55-62`: extension resources can include tools and MCP servers.
112	- `internal/extension/manifest.go:154-160`: extension `ToolConfig` is static metadata only.
113	- `internal/extension/resource_publication.go:13-31`: manifest tool declarations become `toolspkg.Tool` records.
114	- `internal/config/agent.go:14-23`: `AgentDef.Tools []string` is flat.
115	- `internal/store/session_lineage.go:31-39`: session lineage has concrete `Tools` permission atoms.
116	- `internal/session/interfaces.go:244-251`: `AgentDriver` has no catalog-delta or AGH tool injection extension.
117	- `internal/hooks/payloads.go:520-568`: tool pre/post/error payloads already exist.
118	- `internal/skills/registry.go:100-103`: skills expose a global version suitable for catalog delta detection.
119	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/analysis/analysis_skills_tools_registry.md`: prior autonomy gap analysis with G1-G12 and P1-P9 proposals.
120	- `.compozy/tasks/hermes/analysis/analysis_tools_security.md`: security gaps relevant once AGH exposes agent-callable tools.
121	
122	## Design Constraints for the TechSpec
123	
124	The Tool Registry should be a foundation, not a pile of built-in commands. It should define the contracts, policy path, extension boundary, and surfaces first, then add a small bootstrap set of native AGH tools to prove the system.
125	
126	AGH should avoid copying in-process plugin patterns from Python/TypeScript systems. Third-party executable tools should cross a process/protocol boundary: MCP, extension sidecar Host API, subprocess adapter, or future bridge SDK. Built-in Go tools can register in-process because they are part of the daemon binary.
127	
128	The cold `tool` resource should remain valuable as catalog metadata and desired state, but executable dispatch must be modeled separately. A manifest-declared tool can be installed and discoverable while still being unavailable until its backend is healthy and authorized.
129	
130	Availability and authorization must both be rechecked at dispatch time. Hiding unavailable tools from discovery is useful, but it is not a security boundary.
131	
132	## Open Questions
133	
134	1. Should extension tools be executable in the MVP, or should MVP only make them discoverable with an explicit unavailable state?
135	2. If executable, should extension tools be allowed only through MCP/sidecar boundaries, or should trusted bundled extensions get in-process handlers?
136	3. Should the first AGH-native tool surface be injected into ACP sessions, exposed as an MCP server hosted by AGH, or exposed only through CLI/UDS/HTTP for drivers to call indirectly?
137	4. Should `internal/catalog` coordinate tools and skills, or should `internal/tools` own runtime tools while a thinner catalog/search service composes skills and tools?
138	5. Which visibility tiers are needed for MVP: internal, CLI/HTTP, agent-visible, model-visible, deferred-discoverable, extension-private?
139	
140	## Evidence
141	
142	- `internal/tools/tool.go:91-136`: current `Tool` and `ToolProvider` are descriptive and list-only.
143	- `internal/tools/resource.go:13-61`: tool resource codec validates metadata records.
144	- `internal/extension/manifest.go:55-62`: extension manifests can publish `resources.tools`.
145	- `internal/extension/resource_publication.go:13-31`: extension manifest tools become static tool resources.
146	- `internal/daemon/tool_mcp_resources.go:620-640`: daemon sync publishes extension tools and MCP servers into resource desired state.
147	- `internal/store/session_lineage.go:31-39`: session permission policy already includes `Tools`.
148	- `internal/hooks/payloads.go:520-568`: tool lifecycle hooks are already typed.
149	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/analysis/analysis_skills_tools_registry.md:1-220`: prior gap analysis identifies no runtime tool registry, no discovery API, no availability, and no agent-facing skill/tool call surface.
150	- `.compozy/tasks/hermes/analysis/analysis_tools_security.md:1-140`: security analysis warns that URL-capable, command-capable, MCP, and skill-install surfaces require stronger guardrails before broad exposure.
151

1	# Internal Backend (Go)
2	
3	The Go runtime — `internal/*` packages composed by `internal/daemon`, plus the API transports under `internal/api/*`. ACP subprocess management, SQLite persistence, HTTP/SSE + UDS APIs, autonomy kernel, AGH Network. Entry binary lives in `cmd/agh`.
4	
5	Repo-wide rules (Critical Rules, Workflow, Build, Commits, Skill Dispatch, Memory & Skills RFC, CI/Release) live in the **root `CLAUDE.md`**. This file owns architecture, package boundaries, autonomy contracts, security invariants, and `internal/`-specific debugging/forensics.
6	
7	## Architecture
8	
9	### Principles
10	
11	- **Designed for incremental extension** — new capabilities arrive as new packages wired into `daemon/`, without modifying existing packages. Small interfaces + dependency injection. Every capability plan decides which extension points, hooks, capabilities, tools/resources, bundles, registries, bridge SDKs, and docs must be added, updated, or removed.
12	- **Pragmatic Flat with Discipline** — packages under `internal/`, API transports grouped under `api/`, no domain/infra split, no event bus.
13	- **`daemon/` is the sole composition root** — the only package that imports all others. Reconciliation logic running at boot belongs to composition root and is not "legacy support".
14	- **No package imports `daemon/`, `api/`, or `cli/`** — dependencies flow downward only.
15	- **Interfaces defined where consumed** (Go-style) — `session/` defines `AgentDriver`, `acp/` implements it.
16	- **Direct function calls through interfaces** — no event bus, no reflection-based routing, no NATS as inter-package coordination. NATS is permitted **only** inside `internal/network` as the embedded wire transport for the AGH Network protocol; daemon packages communicate via interfaces and the Notifier pattern, never by publishing to subjects.
17	- **Notifier pattern for fan-out** — typed interface for observability and SSE, not a generic bus.
18	- **No back-pointers between packages** — inject callbacks or interfaces.
19	- **Functional options for constructors** — `NewManager(opts ...Option)`.
20	- **Maps for <10 items** — no registry interfaces for small collections.
21	- **File-level organization** within packages — sub-packages only when complexity justifies it.
22	- **CI-enforceable boundaries** — `mage Boundaries` rules prevent import cycles. Update `magefile.go` Boundaries() in the same commit that introduces a new `internal/api/*` subpackage.
23	- **`internal/api/core` is the canonical handler home.** REST/UDS endpoints exist as shared `BaseHandlers` methods; HTTP and UDS only choose registration and authentication. No transport-duplicated parsing/validation.
24	- **Authoritative primitives are exclusive.** When a primitive owns a state transition (`task.Service.ClaimNextRun`, `Spawn`, `EnsureMigration`), no peer package may replicate it. Wake/observe/sweep are allowed; claim/own is not. The mechanical scheduler does not call `ClaimNextRun`.
25	- **Hooks are typed dispatch, not an event bus.** Dispatch at the call site that owns the state transition. Never tail event/log tables to fire hooks. Hooks may deny/narrow/annotate but cannot bypass safety primitives (claim tokens, leases, TTL, lineage, spawn caps, permission narrowing).
26	- **Agent-manageable by default.** User-visible runtime capabilities must expose stable machine-readable control surfaces for agents: CLI verbs with `-o json`/`-o jsonl` where relevant, HTTP/UDS parity when state crosses the daemon boundary, discoverable status/config output, and docs that describe the agent path. UI-only manageability is incomplete.
27	- **No partial-surface completions.** Any change touching a public surface closes the loop end-to-end in one pass: contract → HTTP handler → UDS handler → CLI client → CLI command → extension/config/docs surfaces → tests → docs.
28	
29	### Concurrency
30	
31	Generic Go concurrency patterns (goroutine ownership, channels vs mutexes, `select`/`ctx.Done()` discipline, no `time.Sleep` in orchestration) live in `agh-code-guidelines`. Architectural invariants below are load-bearing for design decisions:
32	
33	- **Goroutines spawned by `internal/session/manager_*.go` MUST be tracked by Manager-owned WaitGroup and joined in Manager shutdown.** Never put goroutine-owned channels in a struct field that another goroutine mutates — use a per-run handle.
34	- **Detached execution lifetime.** Any work that outlives an HTTP/UDS request — prompts, network channel sends, automation jobs — MUST detach via `context.WithoutCancel(ctx)`. Never tie execution lifetime to request lifetime. Expose explicit cancel endpoints (e.g., `POST /api/sessions/:id/prompt/cancel`).
35	- **`context.WithoutCancel` does NOT preserve deadlines.** Re-attach a deadline if needed.
36	- **Subprocess managed-stop** must respect `ctx.Done()` between Shutdown and Wait. Wrap `proc.Wait()` in `select { case <-proc.Done(): case <-ctx.Done(): }`.
37	- **Process-group supervision parity.** Unix uses process groups; Windows uses forced-exit fallback. Always cross-build with `GOOS=windows GOARCH=amd64 go build` before claiming subprocess work complete. Centralize signaling helpers in `internal/procutil`.
38	
39	### Runtime
40	
41	- Single-binary and local-first. Sidecars or external control planes require a written techspec.
42	- Keep execution paths deterministic and observable.
43	- **Daemon runs in background by default.** No daemon should require a foreground terminal.
44	- **`compozy exec` is headless.** `--format text` returns a single string; `--format json` returns a stream of valid JSON objects; the TUI is opt-in via `--tui`. `exec` does not persist artifacts to `.compozy/runs/` unless `--persist` is given.
45	- **Agent operations must not depend on the web UI.** If agents need to inspect, configure, start, stop, approve, claim, release, or repair a capability, the spec must provide a CLI/HTTP/UDS path with structured output and deterministic errors.
46	
47	### Observability
48	
49	- Every domain operation emits a canonical event with correlation keys (`workspace_id`, `session_id`, `parent_session_id`, `root_session_id`, `agent_name`, `task_id`, `run_id`, `claim_token_hash`, `lease_until`, `workflow_id`, `coordinator_session_id`, `scheduler_reason`, `hook_event`, `hook_name`, `spawn_depth`, `actor_kind`, `actor_id`, `release_reason`).
50	- Cover with a coverage matrix test that fails if any required lifecycle path doesn't emit its canonical event.
51	- Append-only event store (`runtime.db`) is the canonical operational ledger; session DBs are projections, not authority.
52	- Live broadcasters publish only after durable append; reconnect/replay uses `after_seq`.
53	
54	## Security Invariants
55	
56	- **`claim_token` redaction is non-negotiable.** Raw `claim_token` (`agh_claim_*`), MCP auth tokens, OAuth codes, PKCE verifiers, and secret bindings MUST NEVER appear in logs, status APIs, settings views, error payloads, channel messages, SSE, web UI, or memory. Use hash forms (`claim_token_hash`) over the wire. Network layer rejects raw `claim_token` in metadata.
57	- **Symlink escape hardening.** Skill sidecars, skill files, managed-extension dependency copies, and bundle install paths MUST verify resolved targets remain inside approved roots. Use `EvalSymlinks` + path-prefix check, not naive joins. Handle macOS `/private/var/folders` quirk (canonicalize source root before containment check).
58	- **Path security helpers.** Filesystem helpers resolving user-controlled or agent-controlled paths use the `sanitizePathKey` + `realpathDeepestExisting` pattern (defenses against null-byte, URL-encoded traversal, Unicode normalization, symlink-escape).
59	- **Identity proof-stripping defense.** In any signed-message processing path (AGH Network v1), an identity in verified format (`nickname@fingerprint`) without valid `proof` MUST classify as `rejected`, not `unverified`.
60	- **External-call timeouts.** Outbound HTTP/network calls MUST use a client with an explicit timeout. `http.DefaultClient` is forbidden in production code paths.
61	- **Load-time security scan.** Every non-bundled skill is scanned via `internal/skills.VerifyContent` on every load (not just install). Critical findings block; warning findings log; info findings log silently. Bundled skills are exempt because `go:embed` provides immutability.

123	## Memory & Skills Runtime (RFC-backed)
124	
125	- **Five-layer skill/memory/agent precedence**: Bundled → Marketplace → User → Additional → Workspace, with agent-local overriding all. Higher precedence wins on collision; an audit trail logs every shadow.
126	- **Memory taxonomy**: `user | feedback | project | reference` types; scopes `agent | workspace | global`. Default write scope declared per agent in `memory.scope`.
127	- **Memory consolidation gates**: Time → Sessions → Lock cascade ordered by computational cost. Default gates: 24h, 5 touched sessions, file-lock. Never replace gates with naive heuristics.
128	- **Lifecycle hooks** (`on_session_created`, `on_session_stopped`) execute in hierarchy precedence then alphabetical order; configurable timeout (default 5s); fail-open semantics (errors logged, never block); JSON over stdin.
129	
130	## Forensic Bug Fixes
131	
132	- **Bug-fix plans open with confirmed reproduction** (timestamp, command, observed evidence) BEFORE listing changes. "I think" or "probably" is forbidden at the top of a fix plan.
133	- **Inactive metadata repair must distinguish startup-pending from crashed.** Sessions in `m.pending` are still starting, not failed.
134	- **Stale ACP session ids must be classified, not propagated.** Convert `Resource not found` to fresh-start fallback.
135

1	# L-005 — Authoritative primitives are exclusive
2	
3	**Class:** Architecture
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (autonomy ADR-004); reinforced 2026-04-26 (autonomy task_11)
5	**Evidence sources:** ADR-004 + task_11 memory + global_runs analysis
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The mechanical scheduler in `internal/scheduler` was tempted to claim runs directly during sweeps and recoveries. That would have collapsed the agent-pull model into a daemon-push model and given two components authority over the same state transition.
10	
11	## Root cause
12	
13	When two components can perform the same authoritative state transition (claim, spawn, migrate, narrow), they will eventually disagree. Either you serialize them (introducing locks, latency, and complexity), or you accept two sources of truth (introducing races and recovery bugs). The clean answer is to pick one authority per transition and force everything else to _observe and notify_ rather than _act_.
14	
15	## Rule
16	
17	> When an authoritative primitive owns a state transition (`task.Service.ClaimNextRun`, `Spawn`, `EnsureMigration`), no peer package may replicate the transition. Wake/observe/sweep are allowed; claim/own is not.
18	
19	## Examples (canonical authorities in AGH)
20	
21	| Transition                    | Authority                            | Allowed peers                                                  |
22	| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
23	| Claim a `task_run`            | `task.Service.ClaimNextRun`          | `internal/scheduler` may wake idle agents; never claims itself |
24	| Spawn a child session         | Daemon-managed safe-spawn API        | Coordinator submits requests; the daemon decides               |
25	| Apply a schema migration      | `internal/store` migrations registry | `EnsureSchema` is forbidden for column changes                 |
26	| Mutate session terminal state | Session manager                      | Channels, hooks, observability emit events but cannot mutate   |
27	| Approve / publish a task      | Operator + manual API surface        | Coordinators receive enqueues; do not auto-approve             |
28	
29	## Operationalization
30	
31	- **Scheduler can wake and sweep, but cannot claim.** `internal/scheduler` issues `scheduler.wake.count`/`no_match`/`lease_sweep.count`/`error` metrics — never `task.run.claim.success`.
32	- **Hooks can deny/narrow/annotate but cannot bypass safety invariants** (claim tokens, leases, TTL, lineage, spawn caps, permission narrowing).
33	- **Coordination channels are NEVER an ownership/status authority.** Channel `status`/`result` messages cannot mutate ownership/terminal state.
34	
35	## Anti-pattern
36	
37	- Adding a "scheduler.\*" hook taxonomy that lets external code claim runs.
38	- Letting the coordinator bypass `ClaimNextRun` through a "fast-path" for trusted runs.
39	- Allowing the network layer to write terminal state via channel messages.
40	
41	## Source
42	
43	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-004.md` — Split Semantic vs Mechanical Scheduling
44	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-009.md` — Hooks First-Class (forbids hook-as-claim)
45	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_11.md` — scheduler scope-creep avoided
46	- `../analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` lesson L4
47

1	# L-007 — E2E harness regressions follow runtime contract changes
2	
3	**Class:** Testing
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-26 (autonomy task_18 QA)
5	**Evidence sources:** task_18 BUG-001/002/003 + global_runs
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The autonomy MVP `make verify` passed. Real-scenario QA via `real-scenario-qa` then surfaced three Playwright/E2E regressions:
10	
11	- **BUG-001** — workspace onboarding race in the web E2E `TC-AUTO-015` test; absent shared workspace-onboarding wait helper.
12	- **BUG-002** — `acpmock` exact-match canonicalization for situation-augmented prompts. Task 04 added a situation-context augmenter that changed the rendered prompt; the deterministic ACP mock fixture matcher still expected the pre-Task-04 shape.
13	- **BUG-003** — Tasks browser E2E asserting an empty Agents-panel state; manual-first publish actually rendered an active run.
14	
15	All three were rooted in tests written against an _older_ runtime contract. None was a production bug.
16	
17	## Root cause
18	
19	When a runtime contract changes — a new prompt augmenter, a different fixture canonicalization, a new manual-first UI state — the deterministic test infrastructure (acpmock fixtures, Playwright selectors, browser fixtures) embeds the _old_ contract. `make verify` passes because tests still hit their old expectations. Real-scenario QA exposes the drift.
20	
21	## Rule
22	
23	> When a runtime contract changes (prompt augmenter, situation context, fixture format, manual-first UI state), the E2E mock and matchers ship in the same PR. Do not let the test infrastructure encode a stale contract.
24	
25	## Operationalization
26	
27	- For ACP fixture work: replace fragile string-matching with structured prompt metadata. acpmock uses typed metadata, not rendered prompt substrings.
28	- For Playwright E2E: add shared wait helpers (`web/e2e/fixtures/selectors.ts`) for workspace onboarding, session creation, manual-first publish. New runtime states require helper updates in the same PR.
29	- Real-scenario QA is the canonical regression net. `make verify` is necessary but not sufficient.
30	- E2E regressions surfaced in the QA pass are NOT production bugs unless they reveal divergent runtime behavior. Fix the test infrastructure, not the runtime.
31	
32	## Anti-pattern
33	
34	- Adding a `time.Sleep(2 * time.Second)` to "stabilize" a flaky Playwright spec.
35	- Loosening an acpmock matcher to substring instead of metadata.
36	- Skipping E2E in the QA pass because "the unit tests cover it."
37	
38	## Source
39	
40	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_18.md`
41	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/qa/verification-report.md`
42	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/qa/issues/BUG-001.md`, `BUG-002.md`, `BUG-003.md`
43	- `.codex/plans/2026-04-17-e2e-confidence-hardening.md` — root-cause plan for the structured-metadata switch
44	- `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` (task_18 findings), `../analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` (autonomy QA section)

1	# L-008 — Schema migrations are required even on fresh DBs
2	
3	**Class:** Persistence
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (Hermes BUG-002, Critical)
5	**Evidence sources:** Hermes BUG-002 + multiple Hermes/autonomy review issues
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The Hermes track widened the `memory_operation_log` table to add `scope`, `workspace_root`, `filename` columns. The change worked on fresh installs because `internal/memory/catalog.go` boots through `storepkg.EnsureSchema`, which created the new shape. But existing databases kept the old five-column table — `EnsureSchema` skipped existing tables — and `agh memory write` failed on upgrade with `no such column: scope`.
10	
11	CodeRabbit flagged it as Critical. The fix: schema migration v6 added through the deterministic migrations runner.
12	
13	## Root cause
14	
15	`EnsureSchema`-style boot reconciliation has a fundamental gap: it creates tables that don't exist but does not mutate tables that do. Any column/index/constraint addition needs a real migration; a migration is required _even when fresh installs already work_, because upgrade is a first-class scenario in alpha.
16	
17	A second contributor: AGH had two schema paths — the global DB used a real migrations registry, while the catalog DB used `EnsureSchema`. Drift between these paths produced the bug.
18	
19	## Rule
20	
21	> Any change to a SQLite column, index, or constraint MUST add a versioned migration in the migrations registry. `EnsureSchema`-style boot reconciliation is forbidden for column changes. Test fresh-DB AND reopen-after-restart paths.
22	
23	## Operationalization
24	
25	- **One schema migration primitive shared by all SQLite databases** (`agh.db`, `events.db`, catalog DBs, automation scheduler state, memory operation log).
26	- **Numbered migrations** are recorded in `schema_migrations`. Idempotent. Wrapped in transactions.
27	- **Test the upgrade path:** every schema change must include a `Test*FreshDB` test AND a `Test*ReopenAfterRestart` test.
28	- **`SQLite recovery code paths must rename or remove `-wal`and`-shm`companions, not only the`.db` file.** (Cross-cutting hygiene: see refac-v2 issue 001 Critical WAL recovery bug.)
29	- **Schema-version bump** lives in the migrations registry — do not encode it in code constants.
30	
31	## Allowed exception
32	
33	In greenfield alpha, a hard-cut rename + table rewrite without compat migration is allowed when:
34	
35	1. The change is documented in the techspec's "Delete Targets" section.
36	2. All callers of the old shape are deleted in the same change.
37	3. Per-developer wipe of local SQLite is acceptable cost.
38	
39	## Anti-pattern
40	
41	- `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS new_columns ...` then expecting the table to grow.
42	- Schema-version constants in Go code that don't match the migrations registry.
43	- Tests that only cover fresh-DB.
44	
45	## Source
46	
47	- `.codex/ledger/2026-04-25-MEMORY-hermes-qa-execution.md` (BUG-002)
48	- `.compozy/tasks/hermes/reviews-001/issue_020.md` (Critical)
49	- `.compozy/tasks/refac-v2/reviews-001/issue_001.md` (WAL/SHM Critical)
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_07.md` (claim/lease schema v7)
51	- `../analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` lesson L1, `../analysis/analysis_local_runs.md` lesson LL-2
52

1	# L-012 — TechSpec without Go interface signatures triggers heavy review rework
2	
3	**Class:** Spec authoring
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 / 2026-04-26 (autonomy techspec vs. release-adjustments comparison)
5	**Evidence sources:** `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` §PRD/TechSpec Quality Patterns
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	Two TechSpecs from the same period delivered radically different review trajectories:
10	
11	- **Autonomy techspec** (`.compozy/tasks/autonomous/_techspec.md`, 706 lines): MVP boundary at top, listed Architectural Boundaries, Go interface signatures pasted as code blocks (`ClaimCriteria`, `ClaimedRun`, `TaskClaimer`, `SpawnOpts`, `PermissionNarrower`), data-model fields with rationale, side-table-vs-JSON decisions explicit, lease invariants enumerated as a numbered list. Eighteen tasks executed cleanly with **one** review round.
12	- **Release-adjustments / qa-review** (no `_techspec.md`, just review-only directories): unresolved review queues persisted across multiple PRs. Tasks 07-09 of autonomy that touched contract-laden interfaces had **exactly one** round of fixes because the techspec gave the implementer no contract ambiguity.
13	
14	The differentiator was not length — it was concreteness. Specs that paste signatures, list fields with rationale, and enumerate invariants leave nothing to interpretation. Specs that describe the same mechanics in prose force the implementer to invent shapes that reviewers then reject.
15	
16	## Root cause
17	
18	Prose-only descriptions produce N implementations, where N is the number of agents that read the spec. Reviewers then converge each implementation toward the implicit intent through review rounds — that is the rework. Code blocks (interface signatures, struct fields, SQL DDL, enum values) are unambiguous; reviewers either approve or reject specific tokens, and the spec author resolves the ambiguity once instead of N times.
19	
20	## Rule
21	
22	> A TechSpec is not ready for review until it carries the **six quality markers**:
23	>
24	> 1. MVP boundary statement at top.
25	> 2. Architectural Boundaries section.
26	> 3. Concrete Go interface signatures pasted as code blocks (not prose).
27	> 4. Data-model field rationale (purpose + shape per new column / frontmatter field / config key).
28	> 5. Side-table-vs-JSON decision stated for every new domain entity.
29	> 6. Lease / safety invariants as a numbered list.
30	>
31	> Specs without these markers are pre-rejected — they will need multiple review rounds.
32	
33	## Operationalization
34	
35	`cy-spec-peer-review` invokes Opus with a six-marker checklist embedded in the prompt. `cy-spec-preflight` blocks `cy-create-techspec` from completing until the six markers are present.
36	
37	When a spec is missing a marker, fix the spec — do not start tasks against the gap.
38	
39	## Anti-patterns
40	
41	- "The function will accept the relevant config and return the result." (no signature)
42	- "Add a column for ownership tracking." (no rationale, no name, no type)
43	- "Choose the appropriate storage shape." (no decision)
44	- "Ensure the lease is held safely." (no invariants)
45	- "We'll figure out the interface during implementation." (defers the ambiguity)
46	
47	## Source
48	
49	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/_techspec.md` — the cleanest exemplar
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-003.md` (lease invariants numbered)
51	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-006.md` (safe spawn caps numbered)
52	- `.compozy/tasks/release-adjustments/` and `.compozy/tasks/qa-review/` — review-only dirs without TechSpecs, persistent unresolved review queues
53	- `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` §"Markers of 'good enough to execute'" and §"Markers of trouble"
54	- `docs/_memory/_synthesis.md` skill candidate S-M9 `agh-techspec-quality-gate`
55	
```

1	# L-006 — Greenfield + zero-legacy means _delete_, not _adapt_
2	
3	**Class:** Project posture
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-17 (harness TechSpec review, Portuguese-language reviewer)
5	**Evidence sources:** Harness review + `remove-legacy-alpha.md` standing directive + multiple ADRs
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The harness TechSpec proposed migrating an `inputAugmenter` callback to a `TurnAugmenter` pipeline. The spec did not say whether the old callback was deleted, kept as an adapter, or coexisting. The reviewer (in Portuguese) flagged this directly: _"política zero-legacy exige declarar 'delete'"_ — the zero-legacy policy _requires_ the spec to declare what is deleted.
10	
11	This is a stronger application of the CLAUDE.md "Greenfield Alpha — Zero Legacy Tolerance" rule: it's not enough to _allow_ deletion; specs must _enumerate_ what is deleted.
12	
13	## Root cause
14	
15	When a spec says "we are migrating to X" without naming the delete-target, agents default to keeping both. Compatibility shims, adapters, and "preserve old behavior" branches accumulate as technical debt. Greenfield discipline only works if every breaking-change spec explicitly names what disappears.
16	
17	## Rule
18	
19	> Every breaking-change techspec MUST explicitly name its delete targets. "Delete the old thing" is not a default; it is a checklist item that must be enumerated.
20	
21	## Operationalization
22	
23	In every TechSpec that changes a public surface (or any meaningful internal contract), include a section like:
24	
25	```markdown
26	## Delete Targets
27	
28	- `internal/foo.OldType` (replaced by `internal/foo.NewType` in step 3)
29	- `pkg/bar.LegacyAdapter` (no callers after migration; remove in step 5)
30	- TOML key `[old.section]` (renamed; no backward alias)
31	- HTTP endpoint `/v0/old/path` (replaced by `/v1/new/path`; no redirect)
32	```
33	
34	Renames sweep code, storage, APIs, CLI, extensions, specs, RFCs, AND `.compozy/tasks/*` artifacts in the same change. No aliases, no dual fields, no migration code.
35	
36	## Allowed exception (single-pass repair)
37	
38	When the cost of "delete the old thing" is "every developer rebuilds their local SQLite," in-place ALTER + one-shot repair is allowed if and only if:
39	
40	1. Repair is bounded to a single boot.
41	2. Strict semantics resume immediately after repair.
42	3. The exception is documented in an ADR.
43	
44	Reference: `session-driver-override/adrs/adr-005.md`.
45	
46	## Source
47	
48	- `.codex/plans/remove-legacy-alpha.md` (standing directive)
49	- `.codex/sessions/2026/04/17/.../exec-20260417-232547-929722000/turns/0001/response.txt` (harness review, Portuguese)
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-001.md`
51	- Multiple `network-rename`, `assistant-ui-hard-cut`, `workspace-menu-hardcut` plans in `.codex/plans/`
52	- `../analysis/analysis_local_runs.md` lesson LL-1
